preamble: The shift from [e] to [h] is typically a result of extreme devoicing or the development of aspiration (rough breathing) before initial vowels. It is historically prominent in the transition from various ancient Greek dialects.

examples.note: Spiritus asper (rough breathing): an [h] appears before the initial vowel. Strictly ∅→h, not e→h; the vowel is retained. Documented in Attic and Ionic Greek.
Japanese unstressed vowels devoice in certain environments; /e/ is less commonly devoiced than /i/ or /u/. True e→h replacement is marginal; flagged as poorly documented.
